title: 13 Best Herbal Teas For Chapped Lips
intro: Herbal teas for Chapped lips are a natural remedy used to soothe and heal dry, cracked lips. These teas are made from various herbs that have anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and moisturizing properties, which help to calm and protect the skin. Some examples of herbal teas that can be used to treat chapped lips include peppermint tea, which cools and soothes the skin, chamomile tea, which has anti-inflammatory properties to reduce swelling, and hibiscus tea, which is rich in antioxidants and has moisturizing properties to hydrate the lips. Additionally, other herbal teas such as calendula tea, aloe vera tea, and green tea can also be used to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and hydrating properties.

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe barbadensis teas, derived from the aloe vera plant, are a popular herbal preparation used to treat chapped lips. The soothing and moisturizing properties of this tea help to treat chapped lips by providing essential hydration and protecting the skin from further dryness. The bioactive constituents, such as aloin, aloe-emodin, and vitamins A and E, found in aloe barbadensis teas, possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that aid in the healing process and protect the skin from damage. By consuming aloe barbadensis tea, individuals can experience the benefits of improved lip health, reduced inflammation, and accelerated wound healing, making it a natural and effective remedy for chapped lips.
preparation_procedure: Get 1 cup of fresh Aloe vera gel from an Aloe barbadensis plant. Cut the leaf to extract the gel.
Add 2 tablespoons of honey to the Aloe vera gel. Mix well to combine.
Add 1/4 cup of boiling water to the Aloe vera gel and honey mixture.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain it through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup.
Drink the tea to soothe chapped lips from the inside. You can also apply the remaining gel directly to your lips for immediate relief.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe barbadensis teas can be beneficial in treating chapped lips, but it may cause some side effects such as allergic reactions, digestive issues, or an interaction with certain medications due to its potential for stimulating the digestive system. When using Aloe barbadensis teas to treat chapped lips, it is recommended to start with small amounts, be cautious with the dosage, and avoid consuming it if you have sensitive stomach or are prone to digestive problems.
preparation_usage: Take 1 teaspoon of Aloe barbadensis tea 2-3 times a day
Soak a cotton swab in the tea and apply to lips
Leave it on for 10-15 minutes to allow absorption
Rinse with water and pat dry with a clean cloth
Repeat daily for 7-10 days to see noticeable improvement

preparations.herb_desc: Calendula officinalis teas have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which help to soothe and protect the affected area. The bioactive constituents, such as triterpenoids and flavonoids, in Calendula officinalis teas work by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing, thereby helping to treat chapped lips. This herbal preparation helps to treat chapped lips by forming a protective barrier on the skin's surface, reducing dryness and irritation, and promoting the regeneration of healthy skin cells. The benefits of using Calendula officinalis teas to treat chapped lips include reduced inflammation, accelerated healing, and long-term prevention of dry and chapped lips.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Calendula officinalis flowers.
Measure 1 tablespoon of the dried flowers and put them in a tea infuser.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Steep the Calendula flowers in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool before applying it to your chapped lips with a cotton swab.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "BMC veterinary research", Calendula officinalis teas for chapped lips may be beneficial due to its antibacterial and antifungal effects, which can help soothe and heal the skin, although the study specifically mentions its use in treating dermatologic diseases in dogs.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Calendula officinalis teas can be beneficial in treating chapped lips, but some individuals may experience side effects such as skin irritation, redness, or an allergic reaction. To minimize potential side effects, it's essential to use high-quality Calendula officinalis teas, patch test the tea on a small area of skin before applying it to the lips, and avoid using it if you have sensitive skin or allergies.
preparation_usage: Measure 1 teaspoon of dried Calendula officinalis flowers.
Steep the flowers in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture and let it cool to room temperature.
Soak a cotton swab in the cooled mixture and apply it to chapped lips.
Repeat the process 2-3 times a day as needed for up to 2 weeks.

preparations.herb_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas, derived from the root of the licorice plant, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and moisturizing properties. The anti-inflammatory compounds present in these teas, such as glycyrrhizin and flavonoids, help to reduce inflammation and promote healing of the affected area. By providing a protective barrier and locking in moisture, Glycyrrhiza glabra teas help to treat chapped lips, making them a popular natural remedy for dry, cracked lips. The benefits of using Glycyrrhiza glabra teas to treat chapped lips include a reduction in discomfort, faster healing, and improved overall lip health.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 tablespoons of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root, 1 cup of water, and a tea infuser or strainer.
Boil the water and pour it over the dried root in the tea infuser or strainer.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to release.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the root. You can add honey to taste if desired.
Apply the cooled tea to your chapped lips using a cotton ball or swab, and repeat as needed.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas can be used to treat chapped lips, but its long-term consumption may lead to side effects such as high blood pressure, potassium deficiency, and an increased risk of heart problems due to its licorice root content. To use Glycyrrhiza glabra teas safely, it is recommended to consume them in moderation, limit the frequency of use, and monitor blood pressure and potassium levels, as excessive consumption can exacerbate these issues.
preparation_usage: Soak a cotton swab in Glycyrrhiza glabra tea.
Dip the swab in the tea 3-4 times.
Apply the tea-soaked swab to your chapped lips.
Leave it on for 5-10 minutes, then wipe off.
Use this remedy 2-3 times a day, as needed.

preparations.herb_desc: Echinacea purpurea teas have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties, which help to calm and moisturize the affected area. The bioactive constituents of Echinacea purpurea, including alkylamides and phenolic compounds, possess antimicrobial and antioxidant activities that aid in healing and protecting the lips from further irritation. By consuming Echinacea purpurea teas, individuals can reap the benefits of a natural remedy that promotes lip health and prevents the recurrence of chapped lips. Regular consumption of Echinacea purpurea teas can lead to improved lip hydration, reduced inflammation, and enhanced overall lip health.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Echinacea purpurea flowers, 1 cup of boiling water, and a strainer.
Steep the flowers in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup using a strainer.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey (optional) to the tea for taste and to soothe chapped lips.
Let the tea cool and use it as a lip balm by applying it to your lips with a cotton swab.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Echinacea purpurea teas can be beneficial in treating chapped lips, but using it may lead to potential side effects such as allergic reactions, skin irritation, and digestive issues in some individuals. Precautions to take when using Echinacea purpurea teas include starting with small doses, avoiding consumption for prolonged periods, and being aware of any interactions with other medications or health conditions.
preparation_usage: Steep 1 teaspoon of dried Echinacea purpurea in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid and let it cool down to room temperature.
Apply the cooled liquid to chapped lips using a cotton swab 2-3 times a day.
Repeat the process for 1 week for optimal results and relief from chapped lips.
For maintenance, use Echinacea purpurea tea 1-2 times a week to keep lips healthy and moisturized.

preparations.herb_desc: Melissa officinalis teas, also known as lemon balm teas, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and moisturizing properties. The herbal preparation helps to treat this ailment by providing a protective barrier on the skin's surface and promoting the healing process through its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Melissa officinalis teas, such as rosmarinic acid, apigenin, and luteolin, contribute to its therapeutic effects by reducing inflammation, fighting off infections, and calming irritated skin. The benefits of using Melissa officinalis teas to treat chapped lips include alleviating discomfort, promoting healthy skin regeneration, and providing natural relief without harsh chemicals or artificial additives.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Melissa officinalis leaves and flowers.
Steep 1 tablespoon of Melissa officinalis leaves and flowers in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a separate cup.
Let the tea cool down to room temperature.
Soak a cotton swab in the cooled tea and apply it to your chapped lips for relief.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Melissa officinalis teas can provide relief from chapped lips due to its moisturizing and soothing properties, but potential side effects may include allergic reactions such as skin irritation or redness, and interactions with certain medications like blood thinners. To minimize risks, it's essential to start with a small dose and gradually increase as needed, be cautious when consuming it with other herbal remedies, and avoid ingesting it in large quantities to prevent any adverse reactions.
preparation_usage: Soak a cotton ball with 1 tablespoon of Melissa officinalis tea.
Gently apply the tea to your chapped lips for 5-10 minutes.
Rinse your lips with warm water to remove excess tea.
Repeat the process 2-3 times a day, as needed.
Continue for 1-2 weeks to see significant improvement.

preparations.herb_desc: Lavandula angustifolia teas have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and moisturizing properties, which help to calm and hydrate dry, cracked skin. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of this herbal preparation, particularly the flavonoids and terpenes, help to reduce inflammation and protect the skin from damage caused by environmental stressors. The bioactive constituents of Lavandula angustifolia teas, such as linalool and linalyl acetate, possess antimicrobial and antifungal properties that help to prevent infection and promote healing of chapped lips. Regular consumption of Lavandula angustifolia teas has been found to provide relief from chapped lips by promoting skin hydration, reducing inflammation, and protecting against environmental stressors, making it a popular natural remedy for this common affliction.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Lavandula angustifolia flowers or 2 tablespoons of dried flowers.
Heat 1 cup of water in a saucepan and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and add the Lavandula angustifolia flowers to the water. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture and let it cool. You can store the tea in an airtight container in the fridge for later use.
Soak a cotton swab in the cooled tea and apply it to your chapped lips 2-3 times a day for relief.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Lavandula angustifolia teas can be used to treat chapped lips, but it may cause mild side effects such as dry mouth, digestive issues, and skin irritation in some individuals. To avoid potential side effects, it's recommended to start with a small dose, avoid consuming it if you have allergies or sensitive skin, and choose high-quality tea leaves from a reputable source to minimize the risk of contamination or adulteration.
preparation_usage: Soak a cotton swab in Lavandula angustifolia tea.
Dip swab in solution 2-3 times a day as needed.
Gently apply to chapped lips for 5-10 minutes.
Rinse with lukewarm water and pat dry with a towel.
Repeat daily for 7-10 days to see significant improvements.

preparations.herb_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis teas have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their moisturizing and soothing properties. preparations.preparation_side_effect: Rosmarinus officinalis teas can be an effective remedy for chapped lips due to its moisturizing and antibacterial properties, however, some individuals may experience side effects such as allergic reactions, skin irritation, or an increase in heart rate. preparation_usage: Measure 1 tablespoon of dried Rosmarinus officinalis leaves.
Steep in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain and let the tea cool down completely.
Soak a cotton swab in the tea and apply to chapped lips 2-3 times a day.

preparations.herb_desc: Mentha x piperita teas, commonly known as peppermint tea, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. This herbal preparation helps to treat chapped lips by reducing inflammation and irritation, promoting moisture retention, and providing relief from discomfort. The bioactive constituents of peppermint tea, including menthol, menthone, and limonene, help to treat chapped lips by creating a protective barrier on the skin's surface and stimulating blood flow to aid in healing. The benefits of peppermint tea in treating chapped lips include its ability to provide rapid relief, promote wound healing, and prevent future occurrences of dry, cracked skin.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of boiling water, 2 tablespoons of fresh mint leaves, and a tea strainer or infuser.
Place the mint leaves in the tea strainer or infuser.
Pour the boiling water over the mint leaves and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and let it cool.
Soak a cotton ball or swab in the cooled tea and apply it to your chapped lips to soothe and moisturize.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Mentha x piperita teas can be an effective remedy for chapped lips, but possible side effects may include stomach upset, allergic reactions, and interactions with certain medications due to its menthol content. To use Mentha x piperita teas safely, it's essential to start with small doses, avoid ingesting large amounts, and be cautious when applying the tea to sensitive skin areas to prevent irritation.
preparation_usage: Steep 1 teaspoon of Mentha x piperita tea in 1 cup of boiling water.
Let the tea cool down to room temperature.
Dip a cotton swab in the tea and apply to chapped lips.
Apply 2-3 times a day, ideally after meals and before bed.
Use for 5-7 days to see noticeable improvement in lip health.

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe vera teas have been used as a natural remedy to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and moisturizing properties. The herbal preparation of aloe vera helps to treat chapped lips by providing instant hydration and reducing inflammation, allowing the skin to heal and regenerate faster. The bioactive constituents of aloe vera, such as aloin and aloe-emodin, have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties that help to calm and protect the lips from further irritation. The benefits of using aloe vera teas to treat chapped lips include its ability to provide long-lasting relief, promote skin health, and prevent future occurrences of dry and chapped lips.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 2 tablespoons of Aloe vera gel in a bowl.
Add the Aloe vera gel to the cup of water and mix well to combine.
Heat the mixture in a saucepan over low heat for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and let it cool down to room temperature.
Once cooled, use the Aloe vera tea as a lip balm by applying it directly to chapped lips.
preparations.preparation_study: According to the study on herbal products for oral health care, Aloe vera teas for chapped lips may be considered an effective and safe alternative treatment due to its reported use in treating various oral diseases and limited adverse side effects.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe vera teas can cause allergic reactions, such as redness, itching, and swelling of the lips and surrounding areas, especially if you have sensitive skin. To use Aloe vera teas safely for chapped lips, be cautious when introducing it to your skin, and start with a small patch test to monitor for any adverse reactions.
preparation_usage: Soak a clean cloth in Aloe vera tea.
Dip the cloth 2-3 times a day for 5 minutes.
Gently press the cloth on your chapped lips.
Leave it on for 10-15 minutes, then wipe off.
Repeat this process 2-3 times a day for 7-10 days.

preparations.herb_desc: Taraxacum officinale teas, derived from the dandelion plant, have been used to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and moisturizing properties. The anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce redness and prevent infections, promoting faster healing of the affected area. The bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, contribute to its therapeutic effects by protecting the skin from environmental stressors and enhancing its natural barrier function. Regular consumption of Taraxacum officinale teas can provide benefits in treating chapped lips, including reduced inflammation, improved hydration, and accelerated wound healing.

preparations.preparation_side_effect: Taraxacum officinale teas can be used to treat chapped lips, but it may cause some side effects such as allergic reactions, skin irritation, or stomach upset in some individuals. To safely use Taraxacum officinale teas for chapped lips, it is essential to start with small amounts and gradually increase the dose, avoid taking it before bedtime, and be cautious with its interaction with other medications, especially blood thinners and diabetes medications.
preparation_usage: Measure 1-2 teaspoons of dried Taraxacum officinale in a cup.
Steep the mixture in 1 cup of hot water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid and discard the solids.
Apply the cooled liquid to your chapped lips 2-3 times a day.
Use this remedy for 3-5 days to see desired results.

preparations.herb_desc: Urtica dioica teas, also known as nettle teas, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their emollient and soothing properties. The mucilages present in this herbal preparation help to form a protective barrier on the skin, reducing dryness and irritation, thereby providing relief from chapped lips. The bioactive constituents, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, in Urtica dioica teas possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to soothe and calm the skin, reducing inflammation and promoting healing. Regular consumption of Urtica dioica teas is believed to provide long-term benefits in maintaining healthy lips and preventing dryness and chapping.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh Urtica dioica leaves, roots, or both.
Combine the gathered plant parts with 2 cups of boiling water in a saucepan.
Let the mixture simmer for 10 minutes. Then, turn off the heat and let it steep for another 10 minutes.
Strain the liquid into a cup using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove the plant parts.
Drink 1/2 cup of the prepared Urtica dioica tea 2-3 times a day to soothe chapped lips, or use it as a topical solution by applying it to the affected area with a cotton swab.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Urtica dioica teas can be used to treat chapped lips due to its anti-inflammatory and moisturizing properties, but it may cause some side effects such as allergic reactions, itching, and increased heart rate in some individuals. To minimize potential side effects, it is essential to start with a small dose, use high-quality and organic teas, and avoid using it if you have any existing allergies or sensitivities to plants in the Urtica genus.
preparation_usage: Drink 1-2 cups of Urtica dioica tea, 2-3 times daily.
Take 1 teaspoon of dried Urtica dioica herb 3 times daily.
Add 1 tablespoon of Urtica dioica tincture to your tea or water.
Use Urtica dioica lip balm or salve 2-3 times daily as needed.
Continue using Urtica dioica for 2-4 weeks for best results.

preparations.herb_desc: Hypericum perforatum teas, also known as St. John's Wort tea, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal properties. preparations.preparation_study: According to Annals of allergy, asthma & immunology : official publication of the American College of Allergy, Asthma, & Immunology, Hypericum perforatum teas are not mentioned for the treatment of chapped lips, but it is mentioned as St. John's wort (Hypericum) which is associated with anaphylactic reactions and exacerbation of allergies and asthma, however, no information was found on it being used to treat chapped lips.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Hypericum perforatum teas can be an effective natural remedy for chapped lips, but they may cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and increased sensitivity to sunlight. To use Hypericum perforatum teas safely, it's essential to start with small doses, avoid taking them during pregnancy or breastfeeding, and be cautious when consuming them with other medications, especially blood thinners and sedatives, as they may interact and worsen side effects.

Dip a cotton swab into the cooled tea and apply it to your chapped lips.
Repeat the process 2-3 times a day to soothe and moisturize your lips.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Acta pharmaceutica Sinica, Hibiscus sabdariffa teas may be beneficial for chapped lips due to their high content of γ-aminobutyric acid, which has potential antioxidant properties and could contribute to soothing and moisturizing the skin.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Hibiscus sabdariffa teas can provide relief for chapped lips due to their natural anti-inflammatory and moisturizing properties, but possible side effects may include allergic reactions, such as redness, itching, or swelling of the lips, as well as gastrointestinal issues like stomach upset or diarrhea. To minimize risks, individuals using Hibiscus sabdariffa teas to treat chapped lips should start with a small dose, gradually increase the amount, and avoid consuming the tea on an empty stomach or mixing it with other herbal preparations.

preparations.herb_desc: Thymus vulgaris teas, derived from the leaves of the thyme plant, have been used to treat chapped lips due to their soothing and moisturizing properties. The herbal preparation helps to treat this ailment by providing an anti-inflammatory effect and creating a protective barrier on the skin's surface, allowing it to heal faster. The bioactive constituents of thymus vulgaris teas, including carvacrol and thymol, contribute to its healing properties by reducing inflammation and promoting the regeneration of skin cells. Regular consumption of thymus vulgaris teas can provide long-term benefits, such as improved skin health and reduced risk of infection, making it an effective natural remedy for chapped lips.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried Thymus vulgaris leaves and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the Thymus vulgaris leaves in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup to remove the leaves.
Add honey to taste (about 1 teaspoon) to the Thymus vulgaris tea.
Soak a cotton swab in the tea and apply it to your chapped lips for relief.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Frontiers in bioscience (Landmark edition), Thymus vulgaris teas for chapped lips may be beneficial due to the essential oils of Thymus vulgaris showing anti-Candida activity, which could help prevent fungal infections that may cause or exacerbate chapped lips.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Thymus vulgaris teas can be used to treat chapped lips, but possible side effects may include allergic reactions, such as skin irritation, itching, or redness, especially for individuals with sensitive skin. Precautions should be taken when using Thymus vulgaris teas to treat chapped lips, including avoiding excessive consumption, as it may cause dizziness or stomach upset, and also avoiding application on broken skin.

preparations.herb_desc: Thymus serpyllum teas, also known as wild thyme tea, have been used for centuries to treat chapped lips due to its antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and moisturizing properties. This herbal preparation helps to treat chapped lips by providing a protective barrier against environmental stressors and promoting wound healing, which leads to faster recovery and reduced discomfort. The bioactive constituents of wild thyme, including thymol and carvacrol, possess antimicrobial and antioxidant properties that help to soothe and calm chapped lips, reducing inflammation and promoting hydration. The benefits of using Thymus serpyllum teas to treat chapped lips include quick relief from discomfort, reduced risk of infection, and a natural, non-irritating solution for dry, chapped lips.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water, 1 tablespoon of dried Thymus serpyllum, and a tea infuser.
Boil the water and pour it into a cup.
Add the dried Thymus serpyllum to the tea infuser and place it in the cup.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes, then remove the tea infuser.
Strain the tea and let it cool before applying it to chapped lips using a cotton ball or swab.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Thymus serpyllum teas can be used as a natural remedy to treat chapped lips, but possible side effects include mild skin irritation, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications, especially blood thinners. To use Thymus serpyllum teas safely, individuals should start with small doses, avoid consuming it in large quantities, and refrain from applying it directly to broken skin to minimize the risk of irritation and adverse reactions.

preparations.herb_desc: Zingiber officinale teas, also known as ginger tea, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to their anti-inflammatory and moisturizing properties. The bioactive constituents of ginger tea, such as gingerols and shogaols, help to soothe and protect the lips from dryness and irritation, reducing inflammation and promoting healing. By increasing blood flow to the affected area, ginger tea helps to nourish and hydrate the lips, making them smoother and softer. The benefits of using ginger tea to treat chapped lips include its natural and non-irritating nature, making it an effective and gentle remedy for this common ailment.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Zingiber officinale root and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the Zingiber officinale root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and discard the root.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea to sweeten it.
Soak a cotton ball in the tea and apply it to your chapped lips for relief.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Zingiber officinale teas can be an effective natural remedy for treating chapped lips, but its use may cause side effects such as stomach upset, heartburn, and diarrhea in some individuals due to its high ginger content. To use Zingiber officinale teas safely, it is recommended to start with a low dose, consume it in moderation, and avoid ingesting it before or after surgery, as it may interfere with blood clotting and cause excessive bleeding.

preparations.herb_desc: Hydrastis canadensis teas, also known as goldenseal, have been traditionally used to treat chapped lips due to its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The bioactive constituents, including berberine and hydrastine, help to soothe and protect the skin, reducing dryness and inflammation associated with chapped lips. By applying goldenseal tea to the affected area, it helps to lock in moisture, promote tissue repair, and prevent further irritation. The benefits of using goldenseal tea for chapped lips include accelerated healing, reduced discomfort, and a decrease in the risk of infection.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Hydrastis canadensis root and 1 cup of water.
Boil the water in a pot and then reduce heat.
Add the dried root to the water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the root.
Let the tea cool down, then use it as a lip balm or apply it to your lips with a cotton swab.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Hydrastis canadensis teas can be used to treat chapped lips due to its anti-inflammatory and moisturizing properties, but possible side effects include skin irritation, allergic reactions, and an increased risk of bleeding when applied topically. To minimize potential side effects, it is essential to use Hydrastis canadensis teas in moderation and avoid applying it to broken skin, as well as to monitor for any signs of skin irritation or allergic reactions during the treatment period.

preparation_study: According to Biomolecules, Aloe barbadensis teas for chapped lips have not been directly mentioned in the provided study, however, Aloe vera is mentioned as a plant with anti-oral mucosal lesion activity, which may suggest its potential benefits for soothing and healing chapped lips.
